old_claim.note: Scientific Peer Review Practices provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Royal Society meetings combined correspondence, observation, experiments, discussion, and publication, so correspondence networks are shared conference infrastructure.

source_shape.source_locator: The Royal Society history page says the Society gathered information by correspondence while Fellows observed, experimented, discussed outcomes, and eventually published results.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source reviews correspondence, observation, experimentation, discussion, and publication as linked Royal Society meeting practices.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Correspondence networks match the meeting content and information flow; peer review is later publication governance and is not the direct conference prerequisite here.

ontology_before: Early Scientific Conferences depended on Scientific Peer Review Practices, overstating same-year publication governance as a direct meeting prerequisite.
ontology_after: Early Scientific Conferences depend on Scientific Correspondence Networks as shared information infrastructure for Royal Society meetings.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: early_scientific_conferences now depends on scientific_correspondence_networks and no longer directly depends on scientific_peer_review_practices.
would_reject_if: The node were rescoped to journal refereeing sessions rather than meetings, demonstrations, correspondence reading, and debate.
A stronger source showed peer review was the direct organizing mechanism for early Royal Society meetings.
Scientific Correspondence Networks were narrowed away from letters, reports, diagrams, or society information exchange.
short_reason: Royal Society meetings used correspondence networks; peer review is not the direct conference mechanism.
